summary: Jeff Atwood recounts his successful adoption of POPFile's Bayesian spam filtering, achieving nearly 99% accuracy after two months. He credits Paul Graham's 2002 essay for popularizing the statistical approach to spam fighting, explaining why it works better than rule-based systems. The post examines how Bayesian filters handle evolving spam tactics like image-only emails and spoofed text. Atwood also evaluates whitelist-based alternatives like SpamArrest, which achieve near-100% effectiveness by requiring human verification, but concludes he's not a fan of burdening senders with verification steps.
key_points: Bayesian filtering assigns actual probabilities rather than arbitrary scores, making the system's decisions transparent and mathematically grounded
The technique considers both spam indicators and innocent words, so legitimate emails containing suspicious terms aren't automatically flagged
Early researchers dismissed Bayesian filtering because they failed to parse headers and HTML, missing crucial spam signals
Whitelist systems with CAPTCHA verification can achieve near-100% spam blocking but impose friction on legitimate senders
Spammers' attempts to defeat content filters (image-only messages, random word insertion) are still detectable through header and HTML analysis
money_quotes: It discovered, of course, that terms like 'virtumundo' and 'teens' were good indicators of spam. But it also discovered that 'per' and 'FL' and 'ff0000' are good indicators of spam.
The problem with a 'score' is that no one knows what it means. The user doesn't know what it means, but worse still, neither does the developer of the filter.
Nonhackers don't often realize this, but most hackers are very competitive.
This type of whitelist delivers damn near 100% effective spam blocking, with no training period required. There's simply no way a machine can pass this test.
Putting the burden of verification on the sender seems kind of onerous to me.
Bayes will always let some spam slip through, so it is arguably the only way to get an ironclad '100 percent' effective spam blocking.
key_insight: Bayesian spam filtering succeeds because it measures actual probabilities rather than arbitrary scores, making the system's reasoning transparent and allowing it to weigh all evidence—both for and against—when classifying messages.
